title: Inspection and Evaluation
narrationScript: Everything the process has done so far ends at a table under a lamp, with your eyes on the part. Inspection is where indications get found, and evaluation is where someone decides what they mean. Both depend on three things being right. The development time, the light, and the discipline of the person looking. Start with the clock. Development needs a minimum, commonly ten minutes, before you trust what you see, because bleed-out takes time to form. It also has a maximum, set by your procedure, because indications never stop growing. Read too early and a tight crack has not bled enough to show. Read too late and the bleed-out spreads so wide it loses its shape and detail. So inspection happens inside a window, and a good habit is to glance at parts as development begins. Watching an indication form tells you things a finished blot cannot. A big flaw bleeds fast and keeps feeding. A shallow one gives a faint stain that barely returns. Now the light, because the rules differ by penetrant type. Fluorescent inspection happens in a darkened booth. A typical requirement puts ultraviolet intensity at the inspection surface at one thousand microwatts per square centimeter or more, and it is measured at the surface with a radiometer, not guessed. Ambient white light in the booth stays at or below about two footcandles, because white light washes out fluorescence. Give the lamp time to warm up and stabilize, and check it with the meter on the schedule your procedure sets. Give your own eyes time too. Walking from a bright shop into a dark booth, you need several minutes of dark adaptation before faint indications become visible. And leave photochromic or tinted lenses out of the booth. They darken under ultraviolet light and rob you of the very signal you are working with. Visible dye is the opposite world. It needs plenty of white light, commonly one hundred footcandles or more at the surface, the same as any fine, close visual work. With time and light correct, you read. Shape is the first language of an indication. A continuous line means a crack, a seam, a lap, or a cold shut. A broken, intermittent line usually means a flaw that is partly closed, often one that has been ground or machined over. Round spots mean porosity or pits, and a cluster of rounds on a casting is the classic porosity picture. Brightness, depth of color, and how fast the bleed-out forms hint at volume. There is a simple field check. Wipe the indication once with a swab barely dampened with solvent, give it a moment with fresh developer, and watch. A real flaw with volume bleeds back. Lint does not. Which brings us to the words that matter on paper. A false indication comes from something other than the part's condition. Lint, a fingerprint, residue from a poor rinse, penetrant transferred from another part. A nonrelevant indication is real but caused by the design. A press fit, a keyway corner, the joint line of an assembly. A relevant indication comes from an actual discontinuity, and relevant indications get evaluated against acceptance criteria. As a Level one technician your scope is clear. You process, you look, you find, you record. Calling a part good or bad belongs to Level two and above, under your employer's written practice. So document like it matters, because it does. Record where the indication is, how long it is, whether it is linear or rounded, single or clustered, and the process details behind it. Sketch it or photograph it if your procedure allows. If handling smears an indication, or you doubt what you see, do not just re-spray the spot. The part gets cleaned and completely reprocessed. The inspector who writes down exactly what was seen, under measured light, inside the window, gives the Level two everything needed for a sound call. That is the job, and the written procedure governs every number in it.
imagePrompt: Inspector in a darkened booth examines an aerospace turbine blade under deep violet UV lamplight, a glowing greenish-yellow linear indication near the blade root, radiometer and logbook on the bench.

sections.heading: The Inspection Window
sections.body:
Inspection happens inside a time window, and both edges matter. The front edge is the development minimum — commonly **10 minutes** — because bleed-out needs time to form; read early and a tight crack simply has not surfaced yet. The back edge is the procedure's maximum, because indications never stop spreading: read hours late and a crisp linear has bled into a wide, fuzzy blob that hides its own shape and merges with its neighbors.

Good inspectors use the window actively. Glance at parts as development begins when you can — watching an indication form tells you things a finished blot cannot. A flaw with real volume bleeds fast and keeps feeding its indication; a shallow, scratch-like condition gives one faint stain and quits. Batch your work so every part is read inside its window, and log the times. A part that misses its window is not re-sprayed for a second chance — it goes back for complete reprocessing.

sections.heading: Lighting for Type I: the Dark Booth
sections.body:
Fluorescent indications are only as bright as the UV-A feeding them, and only as visible as the darkness allows. Typical requirements put **UV-A intensity at 1,000 µW/cm² or more, measured at the inspection surface**, with **ambient visible light at or below about 2 footcandles** — roughly 20 lux — inside the booth. Both numbers come from a meter, not a feeling: a radiometer at the surface for the UV, a calibrated light meter for the ambient check, on the schedule your procedure sets and after lamp changes.

Discipline that makes the booth work:

- **Warm-up.** Let the lamp stabilize before measuring or inspecting.
- **Dark adaptation.** Coming from a bright shop, your eyes need **several minutes** in the dark before faint indications become visible. Build it into the routine — walk in, let your eyes settle, then read.
- **Lenses.** Photochromic and tinted lenses darken under UV and rob you of signal; keep them out of the booth.
- **Lamp condition.** Cracked filters and white-light leakage are findings, not annoyances — fix them before parts are read.

Type II is the opposite world: visible red-on-white wants **at least 100 footcandles** — about 1,000 lux — of white light at the surface, the same as any fine, close visual work.

sections.caption: A fluorescent inspection station with typical viewing values: UV-A measured at the surface, ambient white light held near darkness, and dark-adapted eyes.

sections.title: Numbers at the booth door
sections.body: Typical procedure values: UV-A **≥ 1,000 µW/cm²** at the surface; booth ambient **≤ 2 fc** (about 20 lux); dark adaptation **several minutes**; Type II white light **≥ 100 fc** (about 1,000 lux) at the surface; development **≥ 10 minutes**, read within the procedure's window. These are the bands written procedures typically live in — yours governs.

sections.heading: Reading the Bleed-Out
sections.body:
Shape is the first language of an indication.

- **Continuous linear** — one unbroken line — points to a crack, seam, lap, or cold shut.
- **Intermittent linear** — a broken line of aligned segments — usually means a flaw that is partly closed at the surface, often one that has been ground, machined, or peened over.
- **Rounded** indications mean porosity or pits; a cluster of rounds on a casting is the classic gas-porosity picture.

Behavior adds the second layer. Brightness and how fast bleed-out forms hint at flaw volume: deep cracks feed their indication and keep feeding it. The field check is the **re-bleed**: wipe the indication once with a swab barely dampened with solvent, apply a light pass of developer, and watch. A discontinuity with volume bleeds back through; lint, smudges, and residue do not return. Use the wipe sparingly — once — because every wipe removes penetrant the flaw cannot replace.

Finally, separate discrete indications from background. A weak, diffuse, overall glow is wash quality talking, not a flaw. Discrete bleed-out with a defined shape against a quiet background is the thing you are hunting.

sections.caption: The four bleed-out patterns a Level I must recognize on sight: continuous linear, intermittent linear, rounded, and the streaks and fibers that mark false indications.

sections.heading: True, False, Relevant, Nonrelevant
sections.body:
Every observation lands in one of three bins, and the words carry weight on paper.

- **False indications** come from anything other than the part's condition: lint and fibers, fingerprints, penetrant transferred from another part or a contaminated glove, residue where rinse water pooled in a drainage gully, developer flakes. The tell-tales: they wipe away clean with no re-bleed, sit in patterns that match handling rather than geometry, and often lift off entirely.
- **Nonrelevant indications** are true indications — penetrant really is trapped — but the trap is the design: press fits, keyways, splines, fastener seams, the joint line of an assembly, weld geometry. The giveaway is consistency: the same indication in the same place on every identical part. They are still noted per procedure, because a crack can hide beside one.
- **Relevant indications** come from actual discontinuities and are the reason the test exists. They get measured, classified linear or rounded, documented, and evaluated against acceptance criteria.

The boundary that matters for you: deciding **false versus real** is interpretation; deciding **accept versus reject** is evaluation. At Level I you work to written instructions, flag and record what you see, and pass relevant indications up for evaluation by Level II or III under your employer's written practice.

sections.heading: Documenting at Level I
sections.body:
Documentation is the product the booth ships. A useful record lets a Level II re-find the indication without you in the room:

- **Where:** part number, serial, zone or datum references, distance from identifiable features.
- **What:** linear or rounded, continuous or intermittent, length and width, single or clustered, brightness and re-bleed behavior if checked.
- **How:** penetrant type, method, sensitivity level, developer form, dwell and development times, light readings — the process state that produced the indication.

Mark indication locations only with markers and methods your procedure approves; some materials restrict marker chemistry. If an indication is smeared by handling, or anything about the surface makes you doubt what you see, the answer is never a local re-spray — the part is cleaned and completely reprocessed. When the part is clean, record what was inspected and to which procedure revision; when it carries indications, write what you saw, not what you concluded. Clean, factual records are what make the next pair of eyes effective.

interactive.title: Indication Triage: Relevant, Nonrelevant, or False?
interactive.intro: You are reading developed parts under proper lighting. Classify each observation the way a Level I should call it for the record.
buckets: Nonrelevant — design feature, note per procedure

items.label: Continuous linear bleed-out along a weld toe that returns after a solvent-damp wipe
items.why: A continuous linear indication that re-bleeds comes from a real surface-breaking discontinuity such as a crack — document location and length and refer it.

items.label: Cluster of small rounded indications scattered across a casting surface
items.why: Rounded indications clustered on a casting are the classic surface-porosity picture — a real discontinuity condition that must be recorded and evaluated.

items.label: Crisp line of bleed-out exactly at the joint of a press-fit bushing, identical on every assembly
items.why: Penetrant trapped at an intentional interference fit is caused by the design, not a flaw — nonrelevant, but still noted per your procedure.

items.label: Bleed-out at the sharp corner of a machined keyway, the same on every part in the lot
items.why: Part geometry that traps penetrant produces nonrelevant indications — consistent appearance across identical parts is the giveaway.

items.label: Fuzzy fluorescent band in a drainage groove where rinse water pooled
items.why: Pooled, penetrant-laden rinse water leaves residue unrelated to any discontinuity — a false indication from wash quality.

items.label: Thin glowing fiber that lifts completely away with a swab and never returns
items.why: Lint and fibers hold penetrant and glow, but they wipe away clean with no re-bleed — the classic false indication.

items.label: Fingerprint-shaped fluorescent smudge in the middle of a flat web
items.why: Contaminated gloves or handling transfer penetrant onto the surface — a false indication; reprocess the part if it obscures the examination area.

items.label: Intermittent linear indication along a forging flash line that returns after wipe-off
items.why: An intermittent linear indication that re-bleeds suggests a partially closed discontinuity such as a lap — record it and refer it for evaluation.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of viewing conditions is typical for fluorescent penetrant inspection?
options: UV-A of at least 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface, with ambient white light at or below about 2 fc
UV-A of at least 100 µW/cm², with ambient white light of 100 fc
White light of at least 1,000 lux, with no UV requirement
Any UV intensity, provided the booth is completely dark
checkQuestions.explanation: Typical procedure values require UV-A of 1,000 µW/cm² or more measured at the inspection surface and booth ambient white light held to about 2 fc or less.

checkQuestions.question: An inspector steps from the bright shop floor straight into the booth wearing photochromic safety glasses and starts reading parts. What two problems does this create?
options: None — photochromic lenses clear instantly indoors, and eyes adapt within seconds
UV exposure to the eyes and overheating of the lamp
The lenses fluoresce, and the booth ambient light rises above limits
The eyes have had no dark adaptation, and the lenses darken under UV — both rob the inspector of faint indication light
checkQuestions.explanation: Eyes need several minutes of dark adaptation before faint fluorescent indications become visible, and photochromic lenses darken under UV, cutting the signal further. Both belong outside the booth routine.

checkQuestions.question: What separates a false indication from a nonrelevant indication?
options: False indications are caused by part geometry; nonrelevant ones come from lint and residue
False indications come from something other than the part's condition; nonrelevant ones are real penetrant traps created by the design
There is no difference — both terms describe rejected indications
Nonrelevant indications never need to be noted, while false indications must be evaluated
checkQuestions.explanation: False indications arise from lint, residue, or handling — not the part. Nonrelevant indications are genuine entrapments caused by design features like press fits and keyways, and they are still noted per procedure.

checkQuestions.question: You wipe a linear indication once with a solvent-dampened swab, redevelop lightly, and the line returns. What does that tell you?
options: The indication was lint dragged across the surface
The developer coat was applied too thick
Penetrant is feeding from below — a discontinuity with volume is present
The part was over-washed at the removal step
checkQuestions.explanation: Re-bleed after a single careful wipe means penetrant is still being supplied from inside a flaw. False indications such as lint and smudges do not return.

checkQuestions.question: A Level I finds a sharp continuous linear indication about 3/8 inch long at a weld toe. Per typical written practice, the correct action is to:
options: Buff the area lightly and re-inspect to confirm before telling anyone
Record its location, length, and character, and refer it for evaluation by Level II or III
Reject the part and mark it as scrap
Re-rinse the part to see whether the indication washes off
checkQuestions.explanation: Level I scope is to perform the process, find, and record. Evaluation against acceptance criteria — accept or reject — belongs to Level II or III under the employer's written practice.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §2.6 (inspection and evaluation)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (viewing conditions, general reference)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6, liquid penetrant examination (general reference)
SNT-TC-1A, personnel qualification and certification (Level I scope, general reference)
